pathophysiology_summary: Exercise-associated muscle cramp is a sudden, involuntary, painful skeletal-muscle contraction during/after strenuous exertion, related to neuromuscular fatigue and altered spinal reflex control (with fluid/electrolyte depletion as a contributor in hot, high-sweat endurance activity). It presents as an instantaneous, exquisitely painful palpable knot that eases with passive stretch and recurs on rest — benign and self-limiting, with microtrauma causing 48–72h soreness. The critical ground-truth task is to exclude the limb/life-threatening mimics of acute unilateral calf pain before attributing it to cramp: DVT (gradual diffuse swelling/warmth, risk factors, tenderness at rest), acute compartment syndrome (pain out of proportion, tense woody compartment, paraesthesia, pain on passive stretch of relaxed muscle), acute arterial ischaemia (cold pale pulseless foot), and rhabdomyolysis (dark urine). Here the instantaneous exertional onset, discrete stretch-relieved knot, warm well-perfused foot and absent DVT risk factors point to benign cramp.

differential.evidence_basis: Instantaneous exertional onset with a discrete stretch-relieved cramping knot after a hot long run.
Unilateral calf pain must exclude DVT; onset pattern and lack of risk factors argue against.
Post-exertional calf pain must exclude compartment syndrome; no pain-out-of-proportion/paraesthesia/tense compartment.
Overlaps; no palpable defect and pain fluctuating with spasm argue against a complete tear, though minor microtrauma likely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Cramp may lead, but DVT, compartment syndrome and ischaemia must be actively excluded before reassurance.

differential.evidence_basis: No DVT features/risk factors, no compartment-syndrome or ischaemia signs, warm perfused foot with normal neuro — benign exertional cramp with microtrauma soreness.
Low Wells; no diffuse swelling/risk factors; escalation target if diffuse swelling/warmth develops.
No pain-out-of-proportion, tense compartment or neuro deficit; escalation target if they develop.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative vascular/compartment screen, benign cramp leads; conservative management with limb safety-net and rhabdomyolysis (dark-urine) advice.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; self-limiting; physiotherapy if a secondary strain persists.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final position is benign exertional cramp.

red_flags.description: DVT features — diffuse unilateral calf swelling/warmth/redness, tenderness at rest, VTE risk factors
red_flags.clinical_significance: DVT can embolise (PE); needs Wells scoring, D-dimer/ultrasound.

red_flags.description: Compartment syndrome / arterial ischaemia — pain out of proportion, tense woody compartment, paraesthesia, cold pale pulseless foot
Rhabdomyolysis — dark tea/cola-coloured urine after severe exertion

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing acute unilateral calf pain to cramp without excluding DVT, compartment syndrome and ischaemia.
Not asking about dark urine after severe exertion.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation for cord tenderness / measurement of calf circumference
Compartment pressure / assessment of pain on passive stretch of relaxed muscle
Distal pulse palpation and capillary refill
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Conservative self-care (stretch, electrolyte rehydration, analgesia, relative rest); ED for DVT/compartment/ischaemia features or dark urine.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Missing DVT/PE or compartment syndrome behind a 'cramp'.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Sports medicine consensus: exercise-associated muscle cramps
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Acute management is passive stretching of the affected muscle; address fluid/electrolyte status and fatigue; benign and self-limiting. Exclude vascular and compartment emergencies for acute unilateral calf pain.
